phases.label: Register to Vote
phases.description: Ensure you are on the electoral register to have your say.
voterActions: Register at least 12 working days before poll
Update details if you moved house

phases.label: Polling Day
phases.description: Casting your vote in person, by post, or by proxy.
voterActions: Locate polling station on poll card

eligibility.citizenship: British, Irish or qualifying Commonwealth citizen
eligibility.residence: Resident in the UK (or living abroad and registered as an overseas voter)

glossary.definition: A representative elected to the House of Commons. There are 650 MPs in total.
The specific geographical area an MP represents. Voters only vote for candidates in their own constituency.
The voting system used for UK General Elections. The candidate with the most votes in a constituency wins, even without a majority.
The primary chamber of the UK Parliament where MPs debate and vote on laws.
The second chamber of Parliament, consisting of peers who review and suggest changes to legislation.
An election to choose all 650 MPs. Must be held at least every five years.
A document published by a political party outlining their policies and plans if they win the election.
An election held in a single constituency if an MP resigns or dies during a term.
The independent body that oversees elections and regulates political finance in the UK.
The requirement (since 2023) to show a valid form of photo identification when voting in person.
A method of voting where the ballot paper is sent to the voter's address and returned by mail.
When a voter appoints someone else to cast their vote on their behalf.
The formal end of a Parliament before a General Election is held.
When no single political party wins an absolute majority (over 325 seats) in the House of Commons.
When two or more parties join together to form a government after a hung parliament.
The list of everyone who is registered and eligible to vote.
The formal name for the end of a parliamentary session, which suspends all business until the next State Opening.
An MP appointed by their party to maintain discipline and ensure colleagues vote according to the party line.
The impartial MP who presides over debates in the House of Commons and maintains order.
An MP who does not hold a government or opposition shadow ministerial post.
An independent member of the House of Lords who does not belong to any political party.
The second-largest party in the House of Commons, which challenges the government's policies.
Senior members of the opposition party who mirror the roles of government ministers.
Prime Minister's Questions — a weekly session where the PM answers questions from MPs of all parties.

quickQuestions: Do I need ID to vote in the UK?
How do I register to vote?
Can I vote if I am living abroad?

quiz.question: How many MPs are elected to the House of Commons?
quiz.explanation: There are 650 constituencies in the UK, each electing one Member of Parliament.
quiz.funFact: The 650 seats are being reviewed to ensure constituencies have similar numbers of voters.

quiz.question: What voting system is used for UK General Elections?
quiz.explanation: The UK uses First-Past-The-Post, where the candidate with the most votes wins, regardless of whether they have a majority.
quiz.funFact: This system usually favors larger parties and can lead to strong single-party governments.

quiz.question: Since 2023, what MUST you bring to a polling station in person?
quiz.explanation: Voters in Great Britain now need to show a valid photo ID to vote in person at General Elections.
quiz.funFact: If you don't have an accepted ID, you can apply for a free Voter Authority Certificate.

quiz.question: On which day of the week are UK General Elections usually held?
quiz.explanation: General Elections in the UK are by tradition held on a Thursday.
quiz.funFact: The last time a general election was not held on a Thursday was in 1931.

quiz.question: What happens in a 'Hung Parliament'?
options: No single party has an absolute majority
A second election is held immediately
quiz.explanation: A hung parliament occurs when no party reaches the 326-seat threshold for a majority.
quiz.funFact: The UK had hung parliaments in 2010 and 2017.

quiz.question: What is the maximum term for a UK Parliament?
quiz.explanation: Under the Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022, the maximum term is 5 years.
quiz.funFact: Parliament is automatically dissolved five years after it first met if it hasn't been dissolved sooner.

quiz.question: Who is the impartial official who maintains order in the House of Commons?
quiz.explanation: The Speaker of the House of Commons is an MP who must resign from their party to remain neutral.
quiz.funFact: When a new Speaker is elected, they are traditionally 'dragged' to the Speaker's chair.

quiz.question: Which of these people is NOT eligible to vote in a UK General Election?
options: A member of the House of Lords
A Commonwealth citizen resident in the UK
An overseas voter registered in their last UK constituency
An Irish citizen resident in the UK
quiz.explanation: Members of the House of Lords are excluded from voting in General Elections.
quiz.funFact: Convicted prisoners serving a sentence are also legally barred from voting in the UK.

quiz.question: What is the name for the formal suspension of a parliamentary session?
quiz.explanation: Prorogation ends a session but not the Parliament itself; dissolution ends the Parliament entirely.
quiz.funFact: Prorogation became a major news topic in 2019 during the Brexit debates.
